chunk_summary: Guidelines for improving iron and zinc bioavailability in Indian vegetarian meals focus on managing inhibitors like phytates and enhancing absorption through vitamin C pairing and food processing methods.
learning_objectives: Understand the impact of phytate-rich meals and tea/coffee on iron and zinc absorption in Indian vegetarian diets.
Learn practical strategies to enhance non-heme iron absorption using vitamin C and food processing techniques.
Apply separation rules for tea/coffee and dairy to optimize therapeutic iron absorption in children and adolescents.
key_facts: Phytates in unrefined cereals and pulses reduce iron and zinc absorption significantly.
Vitamin C sources like lemon, amla, guava, and tomato enhance non-heme iron absorption when paired with plant-based iron sources.
Tea/coffee should be avoided during meals and kept 1–2 hours away from iron-rich meals for adolescents.
Therapeutic iron should be separated from dairy by 1 hour before and 2 hours after consumption.
Food processing methods like soaking, sprouting, fermentation, and adequate cooking reduce phytate content and improve nutrient bioavailability.
reference_quote: Phytate-rich meals (unrefined cereals, pulses) reduce both iron and zinc absorption; tea/coffee taken with meals further reduces non-heme iron absorption; and excessive dairy with meals can displace iron-rich foods.
document_key_topics: Iron and zinc bioavailability in vegetarian diets
Impact of dietary inhibitors on nutrient absorption
Enhancing nutrient absorption through food pairing and processing
